state.name: Vermont
state.peak_intrusion_season: March through May during spring denning, with heavy bear pressure in late summer and a rodent and squirrel surge from October into early winter
state.climate_factor: Vermont's long, cold winters drive rodents, bats, and squirrels indoors aggressively, and the state's growing bear population makes spring and fall the busiest seasons for residential conflicts
state.permit_note: Vermont requires a Nuisance Wildlife Control Operator certification for commercial trappers, and bears, bobcats, and migratory birds carry additional state and federal handling restrictions

Addison County.region: west-central Vermont along Lake Champlain and the Green Mountains
Addison County.habitat_type: Champlain Valley dairy farmland to the west, transitioning into the Green Mountains in the eastern half of the county
Addison County.regional_wildlife: the dairy country around Middlebury sees heavy raccoon and skunk pressure on barns and silos, and bear conflicts at residential properties along the mountain edge are routine spring through fall
Addison County.absent_or_rare: open-country species like coyotes are present but less concentrated than in southern Vermont

Bennington County.region: the southwestern corner of Vermont, bordering New York and Massachusetts
Bennington County.nearest_metro: Albany, NY (about 35 miles west of Bennington)
Bennington County.habitat_type: wooded mountains, small historic towns, and the Battenkill valley
Bennington County.regional_wildlife: the Manchester-area vacation homes and second residences have heavy bear and raccoon pressure, and porcupines damage outbuildings and decks throughout the rural towns
Bennington County.absent_or_rare: moose are present but in lower densities than in northern Vermont

Caledonia County.region: the Northeast Kingdom region of northeastern Vermont
Caledonia County.nearest_metro: the region is its own rural hub; Burlington is about 75 miles west
Caledonia County.habitat_type: the Northeast Kingdom's rolling hills, small farms, and large blocks of working forest
Caledonia County.regional_wildlife: the Northeast Kingdom's heavy forest cover supports strong bear, moose, and fisher populations, and rural barns commonly host bat colonies and raccoon families
Caledonia County.absent_or_rare: urban species like Norway rats are uncommon outside the small downtown of St. Johnsbury

Chittenden County.region: northwestern Vermont along Lake Champlain — the most populous county in the state
Chittenden County.nearest_metro: Burlington is itself the largest metro in Vermont; Montreal is about 95 miles north
Chittenden County.habitat_type: the urban Burlington core, dense suburbs, lakeshore neighborhoods, and rural eastern towns
Chittenden County.regional_wildlife: the Burlington-area suburbs have unusually heavy raccoon and skunk pressure for Vermont, and lakeshore properties along Lake Champlain see frequent bat colony issues
Chittenden County.absent_or_rare: moose are uncommon in this populated county compared to the rest of Vermont

Essex County.region: the far northeastern corner of Vermont, the most remote and least populated county in the state
Essex County.nearest_metro: the region is extremely rural; St. Johnsbury (about 35 miles south) is the nearest population center
natural_features: Silvio O. Conte National Fish and Wildlife Refuge
Essex County.habitat_type: vast working timberlands, beaver wetlands, and a handful of small mill towns — the most heavily forested county in the state
Essex County.regional_wildlife: Essex County has some of the highest moose density in Vermont, and bear, fisher, and beaver issues at camps and rural homes are routine year-round
Essex County.absent_or_rare: the population is so small that commercial wildlife services are limited, and many residents handle issues through state biologists or licensed neighbors

Franklin County.region: northwestern Vermont along the Canadian border and Lake Champlain's eastern shore
Franklin County.nearest_metro: Burlington (about 30 miles south of St. Albans)
Franklin County.habitat_type: Champlain Valley dairy farms, the Missisquoi delta wetlands, and forested foothills inland
Franklin County.regional_wildlife: dairy operations across the county have constant raccoon, skunk, and rodent pressure, and the Missisquoi delta concentrates beavers, muskrats, and migratory waterfowl
Franklin County.absent_or_rare: moose are present in the eastern hills but uncommon in the open farmland

Grand Isle County.region: the chain of islands in the northern part of Lake Champlain, the smallest county in Vermont by area
Grand Isle County.nearest_metro: Burlington (about 25 miles south of South Hero)
Grand Isle County.habitat_type: lake-island farmland, summer homes, and small year-round communities surrounded by Lake Champlain
Grand Isle County.regional_wildlife: lakeshore vacation homes have heavy seasonal raccoon and bat pressure, and the islands' goose populations cause frequent property and dock issues
Grand Isle County.absent_or_rare: many forest mammals common on the Vermont mainland are limited or absent on the islands due to the water barrier

Lamoille County.region: north-central Vermont in the Green Mountains, including the Stowe ski region
Lamoille County.habitat_type: ski resort towns, mountain villages, and large blocks of national and state forest
Lamoille County.regional_wildlife: Stowe and the surrounding ski communities have constant bear pressure on residential properties and dumpsters, and vacation homes see heavy bat and rodent activity during winter dormancy
Lamoille County.absent_or_rare: open-country wildlife is scarce given the heavy forest cover

Orange County.region: east-central Vermont along the Connecticut River and the New Hampshire border
Orange County.nearest_metro: the Lebanon-Hanover area in New Hampshire (just across the river from Bradford)
Orange County.habitat_type: rolling hill farms, river-valley towns, and substantial forest in the uplands
Orange County.regional_wildlife: rural barns and outbuildings throughout the hill towns commonly host bat colonies and raccoon families, and bear pressure increases sharply in late summer when natural foods get scarce
Orange County.absent_or_rare: moose occur but at lower densities than in the Northeast Kingdom

Orleans County.region: northern Vermont along the Canadian border, in the Northeast Kingdom
Orleans County.nearest_metro: the region is rural; St. Johnsbury is about 30 miles south
Orleans County.habitat_type: the Northeast Kingdom's lake country, dairy farms, and surrounding forest
Orleans County.regional_wildlife: Lake Memphremagog and Lake Willoughby shoreline properties have heavy seasonal raccoon, bat, and beaver pressure, and bear conflicts at rural homes are routine
Orleans County.absent_or_rare: urban-adapted species are scarce outside Newport's small downtown

Rutland County.region: west-central Vermont along the New York border, including the Killington ski area
Rutland County.nearest_metro: Albany, NY (about 65 miles southwest) and Burlington (about 70 miles north)
Rutland County.habitat_type: the small urban core of Rutland, surrounding hill farms, ski resort developments, and large blocks of national forest
Rutland County.regional_wildlife: the Killington-area vacation homes have constant bear and rodent pressure, and the Rutland city neighborhoods see urban-edge raccoon, skunk, and rat issues
Rutland County.absent_or_rare: moose are present but more concentrated to the north

Washington County.region: central Vermont, including the state capital and surrounding hill towns
Washington County.habitat_type: the small urban centers of Montpelier and Barre, surrounding hill farms, and the Mad River and Camel's Hump mountain country
Washington County.regional_wildlife: the Mad River Valley vacation homes and ski-area properties have heavy bear pressure, and bat colonies in the older homes of Montpelier and Barre are a recurring summer issue
Washington County.absent_or_rare: moose are present in the higher mountain country but not in the populated valleys

Windham County.region: southeastern Vermont along the Connecticut River and the Massachusetts border
Windham County.nearest_metro: Brattleboro is its own small hub; Springfield, MA is about 60 miles south
Windham County.habitat_type: river-valley towns, ski resort developments around Mount Snow and Stratton, and surrounding mountain forest
Windham County.regional_wildlife: the Mount Snow and Stratton vacation-home communities have constant bear and bat pressure, and Brattleboro's older neighborhoods see heavy urban-edge raccoon and skunk activity
Windham County.absent_or_rare: moose are present but in lower numbers than in northern Vermont

Windsor County.region: east-central Vermont along the Connecticut River, including the Woodstock and Quechee areas
Windsor County.nearest_metro: the Lebanon-Hanover area in New Hampshire (just across the river from White River Junction)
Windsor County.habitat_type: river-valley towns, the affluent Woodstock and Quechee area, ski resorts at Killington's eastern edge and Okemo, and surrounding mountain forest
Windsor County.regional_wildlife: the Woodstock-area vacation homes and Okemo-region ski properties have heavy bear and bat pressure, and rural barns throughout the county commonly host raccoon and squirrel intrusions
Windsor County.absent_or_rare: moose are present in the higher elevations but uncommon in the populated river valley
